skill.name: advanced-evaluation
skill.description: Build LLM-as-judge evaluation workflows with direct scoring, pairwise comparison, rubric design, and bias mitigation guidance.
skill.summary: Design reliable LLM evaluation systems with rubrics, comparisons, metrics, and bias checks.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many weak-crypto and command-execution alerts, but reviewed locations are Markdown examples, JSON examples, or local demonstration code. No evidence found of credential access, external command execution, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts; documentation links are the only network-related content.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only External Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are external research references in SKILL.md, not code that performs network requests. This is a minor marketplace review concern only because users may follow third-party links.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in a References section and are not used by executable code. No network client or request logic was found at these locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Alerts
dangerous_patterns.description: Weak-cryptography alerts match evaluation vocabulary such as scoring criteria, confidence, and examples. Reviewed locations do not implement hashing, encryption, signatures, or password handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain descriptive evaluation content or static example data. There is no cryptographic API usage or security-sensitive key material.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Shell Execution Alerts
dangerous_patterns.description: Ruby or shell backtick alerts are caused by Markdown fenced code blocks. The examples describe Python evaluation logic and do not invoke a shell or execute user-controlled commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is Markdown formatting for examples. No subprocess, os.system, eval, exec, or shell backtick execution was found in the reviewed implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Dangerous Combination Heuristic
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical heuristic combines documentation examples, reference links, and static JSON content. No evidence found of credential access, code execution with network transfer, or obfuscated behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill contains local print-based demonstration code and research links only. Searches for credential terms, network clients, command execution, and prompt injection indicators found no confirming evidence.

content.value_statement: LLM output evaluation is often inconsistent and hard to compare across teams. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured methods for scoring, comparison, rubrics, and bias checks.
actual_capabilities: Design direct scoring prompts with weighted criteria and evidence requirements.
Compare two model responses with position swapping to reduce bias.
Create domain-specific rubrics with score levels and edge case guidance.
Select evaluation metrics such as F1, kappa, rank correlation, and agreement rate.
Plan panel, hierarchical, and human review workflows for higher-stakes evaluations.
Identify position, length, verbosity, authority, and self-enhancement bias risks.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete hosted evaluation service.
Actual scoring still requires a model provider, dataset, and evaluation runner.
Automated judgments must be validated against human review for high-stakes use.
Bias mitigation reduces risk but cannot prove every judgment is correct.

use_cases.title: Compare Model Releases
use_cases.description: Use pairwise comparisons and agreement metrics to decide whether a new model version improves output quality.

use_cases.title: Build Review Rubrics
use_cases.description: Create clear scoring levels for accuracy, clarity, completeness, safety, or domain-specific quality requirements.

use_cases.title: Monitor Production Quality
use_cases.description: Track automated scores, human spot checks, confidence, and bias indicators across deployed AI workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Score One Response
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this response against accuracy, clarity, and completeness. Use a 1-5 scale. Cite evidence before each score. Response: {response}

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Responses
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Response A and Response B for this prompt: {prompt}. Ignore response order and length. Choose A, B, or tie with reasons.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Rubric
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 1-5 rubric for {criterion} in {domain}. Include level descriptions, observable characteristics, and edge case guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Bias And Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this evaluation setup: {evaluation_plan}. Check position, length, verbosity, authority, and self-enhancement bias. Recommend validation metrics.

output_examples.input: Evaluate a beginner explanation of quantum entanglement for accuracy, clarity, and completeness.
output: Accuracy receives a strong score because the analogy describes correlated outcomes, but it simplifies the physics.
Clarity receives the highest score because the language fits a general audience.
Completeness is moderate because the explanation omits measurement context and quantum state details.

output_examples.input: Compare two responses to a machine learning explanation prompt.
output: The analogy-based response wins for accessibility and beginner fit.
The technical response is more formal but uses terms that need explanation.
A second swapped-position pass should confirm the winner before final reporting.

output_examples.input: Create a rubric for code readability in software engineering.
output: Low scores describe unclear names, missing structure, and hard-to-follow logic.
Middle scores describe understandable code with some inconsistent style or organization.
High scores describe clear naming, modular structure, and helpful documentation.

best_practices: Require evidence and justification before every score.
Swap response order during pairwise comparisons and map the result back.
Validate automated evaluation against human judgments before using it for important decisions.
anti_patterns: Using one vague quality score for several unrelated criteria.
Running a single pairwise comparison without checking position bias.
Treating LLM judge results as ground truth without calibration or review.

faq.question: What is this skill best for?
faq.answer: It is best for designing repeatable LLM evaluation workflows, scoring rubrics, model comparisons, and bias checks.

faq.question: Does it run evaluations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides prompts, patterns, and example logic. You still need an evaluation runner and model access.

faq.question: Can it compare outputs from different models?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains direct scoring, pairwise comparison, position swapping, and win rate reporting.

faq.question: How does it reduce judge bias?
faq.answer: It recommends position swapping, length controls, cross-model judging, blind review, and evidence-based scoring.

faq.question: Which metrics does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers precision, recall, F1, kappa, rank correlation, agreement rate, and position consistency.

faq.question: Is it suitable for high-stakes evaluation?
faq.answer: Use it with human review, validation data, and confidence thresholds for high-stakes decisions.
